title: England's Successful Post-Brexit Farming Reforms
source: The Guardian - The Economist (Web Edition) 28 March 2026
content:
A common belief among farmers, though usually uttered quietly, is that their neighbors could always be doing a better job managing their land. The same spirit of competitive one-upmanship can easily be applied to entire countries and their agricultural policies. Some nations simply have better farming policies than others, wasting less public money while boosting productivity and reducing pollution. Surprisingly, England has recently earned the right to look down on others regarding its innovative agricultural reforms. Brexiteers who claimed that leaving the European Union would lead to superior policies have been proven right in the specific realm of farming.

Under the EU’s Common Agricultural Policy, Britain historically paid farmers simply to farm, handing out generous subsidies based purely on land area. While Northern Ireland, Scotland, and Wales still retain many of those traditional area-based payments, England has taken a radically different approach. England has remorselessly slashed these per-hectare subsidies, and they are scheduled to vanish entirely by next year. To receive public money now, English farmers must actively choose to perform tasks that provide clear public goods. These required activities include establishing natural hedgerows, restoring wetlands, or growing specific plants that feed local insects and birds.

While "agri-environment" schemes have existed for years across Europe, England is unique in redirecting almost all of its farm payments toward them. In stark contrast, only a quarter of farm payments in the EU go to environmental schemes of this sort. Although it is too early to tell if England’s approach is definitively boosting biodiversity, the early signs are highly encouraging. Livestock numbers are gradually falling, tree-planting initiatives are rising, and more agricultural land is being left fallow to recover. Crucially, the resulting blow to national food production is likely to be small, as farmers are primarily putting their worst, least productive land into these environmental schemes.

Fears that farmers would be unable to cope financially with the sudden loss of traditional subsidies have largely proved groundless. Only a small fraction of England’s roughly 100,000 farmers chose to take government grants to quit the industry altogether. Profits have generally held up, partly because farmers have learned to apply for environmental grants to supplement their incomes. Additionally, farmers are making better, more diverse use of their land by renting fields to solar-power producers and leasing cottages to holidaymakers. The removal of simple per-hectare subsidies has undeniably made farming riskier, which explains why the price of ordinary arable land in England has cooled compared to Scotland and Wales.

This cooling land market actually benefits good farmers in England, making it easier for them to expand their operations or acquire land for the first time. While the new system is largely successful, critics suggest the government could be bolder by paying for specific environmental outcomes, such as reduced flooding, rather than just activities. England’s remarkable feat is having herded its agricultural sector into a completely new system of environmental incentives at high speed and with minimal disruption. Although some angry farmers have protested in Westminster recently, their grievances were primarily over inheritance tax changes rather than the subsidy cuts. Ultimately, England’s gentle but firm approach to agricultural reform may prove far more fertile than more drastic measures taken elsewhere.
